7 min read (not including linked documents)
0:00 / 7:12
‘Project X’ is our internal name (for now) for the example business
we’ll create following the framework we’ve developed for The
Durable Business.
We’ll turn the idea into a business that we’ll grow from $0 (where
we are now) to $100,000+ in revenue, and you’ll see what we do
every step of the way.
We’ve started this project with nothing — no list, no website, no
advertising accounts, no audience, no offer, and, most
importantly, no idea.
And, to be fair, we’re going to create this business outside the
internet marketing space, so we don’t have any implicit
advantages from our reputations and experience.
(We’ve even considered changing our names for Project X;
Tweedledum and Tweedledee, Batman and Robin, or just Bob and
Bill?)
Finally, we have one more (signi�cant) challenge — instead of one
of us going through the Discover Process to identify interests,
experiences, and skills, we’re going through the process together,
collaboratively. That adds an interesting layer of complexity (don’t
try this at home, folks!).
Project X: Part I
MP3 File 2.47 MB
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 7 --

We never said this would be easy. (We seem to have a high pain
threshold for seeking out discomfort, which is likely not normal.
We should probably see a professional about this at some point.)
As a refresher, these are the questions we answered; reworded as
�rst person plural (from Discover: Part I):
▪ What interests us?
▪ What skills have we learned, what hobbies have we pursued,
what experiences have we had?
▪ How do we choose to spend our free time; what consumes a
large part of our mindspace?
▪ What would our calendar and credit card statements say
about our interests? (Example: our Amazon book-buying
habits reveals a lot about us — 28 book purchases so far in
January by André for �ction and non-�ction — and 12 non-
�ction book purchases by Shawn.)
▪ How do we spend our time, attention, and discretionary
money?
The answers to those questions were captured on three lists:
�. Our skills.
�. Our hobbies/interests.
�. Our experience (professional and personal).
We have many shared interests, skills, and experiences. We’re
fortunate to share deeply held values too. When we put all of that
together, we kept coming back to a central theme: creativity.
But not just creativity in general — that’s way too vague.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 7 --

Speci�cally, we’re fascinated by the creative process — the
occasionally frustrating and often magical experience of turning
raw materials into new ways of thinking about and
understanding the world around us (and sharing those ideas in
our work and with our audiences).
When we asked “why” repeatedly at every turn, we eventually
revealed a core driver that resonates deeply for both of us:
What is it that we do intuitively and subconsciously to guarantee a
steady �ow of new, high-quality ideas to learn, understand, implement,
and teach?
The ‘subconsciously’ part is an interesting component because it
shines a light on mechanisms that are just outside of our
conscious awareness and, very likely, our understanding of why a
behavior works for us. Both of us are in�nitely curious, so no
doubt this alone is going to pull us to want to discover and learn
more about what we don’t already know and yet understand.
“Do” is the most important word in that sentence. Our careers and
livelihoods depend on our ability to generate and execute
interesting and unique ideas consistently, day after day, year after
year.
By Steven Press�eld’s de�nition, we are professionals, meaning we
show up for work knowing that we’ll �nd our inspiration when
we arrive — as opposed to his de�nition of amateurs who wait for
inspiration to arrive before they show up to work.
After identifying that starting place, we immediately realized how
easy it is to jump ahead to Validation. Ideas for solutions and
offers emerged almost immediately, a pulling force we need to
wrestle with and push back on until later in the process).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 7 --

However, the Discover Process begins with our skills, interests,
and experiences. Before we move on to potential solutions, we
need to �nd audiences who could bene�t from what we
already know.
We started our audience research by identifying books about
creativity that we’ve found most valuable, including:
▪ The Breakout Principle (Herbert Benson, M.D. & William
Proctor)
▪ The War of Art (Steven Press�eld)
▪ A technique for producing ideas (James Webb Young)
▪ The MacGyver Secret (Lee David Zlotoff)
▪ The Inner Game of Work (Timothy Gallwey)
▪ Big Magic: Creative Living Beyond Fear (Elizabeth Gilbert)
▪ Creativity: Flow and the Psychology of Discovery and
Invention (Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi )
▪ Disciplined Dreaming: A Proven System to Drive
Breakthrough Creativity (Josh Linkner)
Once we had our initial list, we scanned Amazon reviews looking
for anything that captured our attention.
▪ What did reviewers complain about?
▪ How did they describe who they thought the ideal audience
would be for the books?
▪ What did they think was really good?
▪ What did reviewers say was missing?
We captured (and will continue to capture) those excerpts in a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 7 --

Google Doc named Project X Audience Research.
That document will expand and evolve over time. Reviewers will
mention other books we’ll add to the list (and we’ll read those
reviews for insights as well).
Once we reached critical mass with our audience research, we
began to notice themes emerge. Initially, we captured those themes
in another document in a list of anything interesting we noticed.
Over time we organized that list by Jobs, Pains, and Gains. We
named that document Project X — Themes from Audience
Research.
Both of those are living documents — they’ll likely be different
every time you look at them. Your documents don’t need to look
the same, but they should contain similar information.
Your sources may be different — for example, Facebook group
comments instead of Amazon reviews — but the general idea is
the same. We’re capturing the language of our potential audience
to better understand what they hope to gain, avoid losing, and
want to accomplish.
We don’t know (yet) who our initial Minimum Viable Audience
will be. “People interested in creativity” is way too broad.
However, we believe that our MVA exists within that larger
audience, and we’re looking for clues to �nd them.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 7 --

There is a delicate balance between enough research to stack
probabilities in our favor and too much research that turns into
navel-gazing procrastination. There isn’t a uniform answer to
know where the balance is, so use your best judgment.
We’ve started to see patterns emerge, and we’re beginning to
develop some ideas for an MVA. However, there’s more research
to do.
Our next steps are:
▪ Finishing mining book reviews for potential jobs, pains,
gains, and other insights.
▪ Pulling on any interesting threads in reviews to other books,
we should consider and mine those reviews as well.
▪ Narrow down our large audience to a smaller subset based
on a broad collection of the jobs, pains, and gains that
interest us most.
▪ Find Facebook and LinkedIn groups for that smaller
audience and research their posts, articles, and comments.
▪ Update our audience research and themes documents.
▪ Reach out to potential prospects for 1-1 interviews (Zoom
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 7 --

and email).
▪ Continue to compile our research until we feel like we’re
ready to clearly de�ne our initial MVA.
We’ll describe our process and results in the forthcoming Project
X: Part II.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 7 --
